framework.reasoning: This paper describes statistical/econometric scenario-generation methods (block resampling, VAR(1) estimation, Nelson-Siegel curve fitting) with no neural network components. The natural implementation stack is numpy/pandas/scipy/statsmodels, not a deep-learning framework. PyTorch is nonetheless declared as the nominal 'framework' field (required by schema enum) and is used narrowly and optionally: batching the 10^4 Monte Carlo trajectory simulations as vectorized tensor operations can benefit from PyTorch's batched linear algebra and optional GPU acceleration for the VAR(1) recursions across many simulated paths. All statistical estimation (OLS for VAR(1), least-squares Nelson-Siegel fitting, KDE, KL divergence) is implemented with numpy/scipy/statsmodels; PyTorch is not required to run any script and every module has a pure-numpy fallback path (config flag 'engine: numpy|torch').

module_hierarchy.description: Package init, version, and top-level exports of the three scenario generators.
Loads and prepares the three historical series (Equity EUR, 1y Italian rate / BOT proxy, HICP ex-tobacco inflation) and the full Italian yield curve panel, at daily and monthly frequency.

module_hierarchy.description: YAML config loading/validation, seeding, engine (numpy/torch) selection.

tensor_flows.name: StationaryBootstrap.simulate
steps: z_hist: [T, n] <- historical joint vector (returns r_t concatenated with rate differences delta_y_t)
for each of n_paths simulated trajectories:
draw a geometric-block resampled index sequence of length `horizon` over rows of z_hist (Politis-Romano stationary bootstrap)
z_star[path] = z_hist[index_sequence] -> [horizon, n]
split z_star into r_star [horizon, n_x] and delta_y_star [horizon, n_y]
reconstruct price levels: P_hat_t = (1 + r_star_t) elementwise* P_hat_{t-1}, cumulative over horizon -> [horizon, n_x]
reconstruct rate levels: y_hat_t = y_hat_{t-1} + delta_y_star_t, cumulative over horizon -> [horizon, n_y]
return stacked simulated levels: [n_paths, horizon, n_x + n_y]

tensor_flows.name: VARBootstrap.fit_and_simulate
steps: x_hist: [T, n] <- state vector (price returns r_t concatenated with rate levels y_t)
estimate a0 [n], A1 [n, n] via OLS regression of x_t on [1, x_{t-1}] across T-1 observations
compute historical residuals eta_hat_t = x_t - a0 - A1 @ x_{t-1} -> [T-1, n]
check stationarity: rho(A1) = max(|eigvals(A1)|); assert rho(A1) < 1 for mean-reverting behavior
for each of n_paths simulated trajectories:
x_hat_0 = x0 (user-supplied or last historical observation)
for t in 1..horizon:
eta_star_t = resample_with_replacement(eta_hat) -> [n]  (or block-resample if resample_block_length > 1)
x_hat_t = a0 + A1 @ x_hat_{t-1} + eta_star_t -> [n]
reconstruct price levels from returns subvector; take rate levels subvector directly
return stacked simulated levels: [n_paths, horizon, n]

tensor_flows.name: NelsonSiegel.fit_factors
steps: curve_panel: [T, M] <- observed rates y_t(tau) at M maturities
calibrate lambda by grid/nonlinear search minimizing MSE over history restricted to tau <= 5y
build loading matrix ell(tau) = [1, L1(tau; lambda), L2(tau; lambda)] -> [M, 3]
for each date t: solve least-squares beta_t = argmin_b || y_t - ell @ b ||^2 -> [3]
compute fitting residuals epsilon_t(tau) = y_t(tau) - ell(tau) @ beta_t -> [M]
return beta_t [T,3] and epsilon_t [T,M]

tensor_flows.name: NelsonSiegelVARBootstrap.simulate_curves
steps: beta_hist: [T, 3] <- Nelson-Siegel factor series from NelsonSiegel.fit_factors
estimate a0 [3], A1 [3,3] via OLS VAR(1) on beta_hist (reuses VARBootstrap.fit logic with n=3)
compute residuals eta_hat_t [T-1, 3]
for each of n_paths simulated trajectories:
beta_hat_0 = beta0 (last historical factor vector)
for t in 1..horizon:
eta_star_t = resample_with_replacement(eta_hat) -> [3]
beta_hat_t = a0 + A1 @ beta_hat_{t-1} + eta_star_t -> [3]
y_hat_t(tau) = ell(tau) @ beta_hat_t for all M maturities -> [horizon, M]
return stacked simulated curves: [n_paths, horizon, M]

model.engine: numpy  # or 'torch' for batched GPU simulation of the VAR recursions

stationary_bootstrap.mean_block_length: ASSUMED — SIR confidence 0.4, see ambiguity 'block-length distribution parameter'; default 20 (trading days) # ASSUMED: literature default (Politis-Romano heuristic order sqrt(T) to T^(1/3))

var_bootstrap.residual_resampling: iid  # ASSUMED: confidence 0.65, see ambiguity 'residual resampling i.i.d. vs block'

nelson_siegel.lambda_calibration: grid_search_mse  # ASSUMED: confidence 0.6, exact numeric lambda not reported in paper

series.equity_eur: data/raw/equity_eur.csv  # MSCI Europe Net TR EUR Index, proprietary — ASSUMED path, user must supply
series.rate_1y: data/raw/bot_1y.csv  # 12m Italian BOT gross yield, proprietary — ASSUMED path
series.inflation: data/raw/hicp_ex_tobacco.csv  # Euro area HICP ex-tobacco, monthly, proprietary — ASSUMED path

data.sample_window: full_history  # ASSUMED: confidence 0.4, exact start/end dates not reported in paper

evaluation.kl_kde_bandwidth: silverman  # ASSUMED: confidence 0.4, paper notes result depends on bandwidth but does not specify it

runtime.reason: core numerical arrays, linear algebra for VAR(1) and Nelson-Siegel least squares
time series loading, alignment, resampling to daily/monthly frequency
least-squares optimization for Nelson-Siegel factors, gaussian_kde for KL divergence
VAR(1) estimation utilities and diagnostic checks
optional batched/GPU-accelerated simulation engine for the 10^4-path Monte Carlo VAR recursions (engine='torch')
reproducing paper figures (Figures 1-4): factor sensitivity, trajectory fan charts, yield-curve snapshots

entrypoints.purpose: Fit Stationary Bootstrap, VAR-Bootstrap, and Nelson-Siegel VAR-Bootstrap on historical data and persist fitted parameters.
Generate n_paths simulated trajectories / yield curves for a chosen methodology over the specified horizon.
Compute Section 6/8 comparison metrics (negative increments, correlation, KL divergence) and Appendix B arbitrage checks; reproduce Tables 1-11.

cli_args.help: Path to simulations.npz produced by simulate.py
Path to historical panel used as ground truth

entrypoints.purpose: Reproduce paper figures: Nelson-Siegel factor sensitivity (Fig.1), Stationary vs VAR-Bootstrap fan charts (Figs.2-3), simulated yield curve snapshots (Fig.4).

risk_assessment.description: The three empirical series (MSCI Europe Net TR EUR, 12m Italian BOT yield, Euro-area HICP ex-tobacco) and the full Italian yield curve panel are proprietary / vendor data (e.g. Bloomberg/Refinitiv), not bundled with the paper. Faithful reproduction of the exact numeric tables (1-11) requires the same data and sample window.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Ship data-loading interfaces that accept user-supplied CSVs matching a documented schema; include a synthetic-data generator (data/make_synthetic.py) so the pipeline is runnable end-to-end without proprietary data, clearly labeled as illustrative rather than reproducing paper's exact figures.

risk_assessment.description: The Nelson-Siegel decay parameter lambda's calibrated numeric value and the exact optimization routine (grid vs. nonlinear solver) are not reported (SIR confidence 0.6, ambiguity flagged).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement calibrate_lambda() with a configurable grid-search default over a wide, documented range and expose it as a config parameter so users can plug in the paper's true lambda if it becomes known.

risk_assessment.description: KL divergence values in Tables 7-9 depend materially on KDE bandwidth choice, which the paper explicitly flags as unspecified.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Default to Silverman's rule via scipy.stats.gaussian_kde, but expose bandwidth as a config flag and report KL divergence sensitivity across 2-3 bandwidth choices in evaluate.py output.

risk_assessment.description: Whether VAR-Bootstrap/NS-VAR-Bootstrap residuals are resampled i.i.d. or in blocks (to preserve volatility clustering) is ambiguous; this materially affects simulated tail behavior and negative-increment intensity metrics (Tables 3-4).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement both i.i.d. and block resampling as an explicit `resample_block_length` config parameter (default 1 = i.i.d.) rather than hardcoding one choice.

risk_assessment.description: Stationary Bootstrap mean block length (geometric distribution parameter) is not reported numerically.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Default to a documented heuristic (e.g. mean block length ~ T^(1/3)) exposed as a config value; document clearly as an assumption in README and config comments.

risk_assessment.description: Numerical stability of Nelson-Siegel loadings L1(tau), L2(tau) as tau -> 0 involves a 0/0 form (1 - e^{-lambda*tau})/(lambda*tau) that needs a stable limit implementation.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement loadings() with a Taylor-series fallback (L1(0) = 1, L2(0) = 0) for tau below a small epsilon threshold, avoiding direct division by near-zero lambda*tau.
